Homer: The Iliad

1.3.1 Definition of An  Epic All literatures have a common focus to reveal the essential reality of life, to discover the ultimate truth and to embrace the unity of the human spirit. Whenever we speak of the classics or the classical canon, we refer to literature that stays timeless and universal. The Ramayana and the Mahabharata, composed approximately between 1३३IBC and ८२७ BC, continue to have an irresistible appeal for us even today, almost after ३३५० years. The epic is one of the classical forms of writing that according to Kline, enlarges the human spirit and shows the grandeur and sublimity of life Epic poems are celebrated for their exalted themes and elevated forms of expression. The grandeur of the content is matched by the grandeur of form in which it is expressed. Sri Aurobindo writing about the epic poem says, the song of the greatest flight that will reveal from the highest pinnacle and with the largest field of vision, the destiny of the human spirit and the presence and ways and purpose of the Divinity in man and the universe (Redfield,  19९४) All epics follow a similar pattern extolling human achievement and the essential dignity of Man even in his most trying moments, in a world that is presided over by Divinity or the Supreme Power C M Bowra writing about the epic poets saysः

Each had his own approach, his own solution, and his own doubts and reservations, but because all were concerned with ultimately the same issues and used the same kind of poetical form, their labours belong to A single chapter in the history of the human spirit. (Hamner 2008)

From all these statements given above, it can be concluded that an epic celebrates the presence of the Divine in the human spirit. २S Let us look at the Ramayana and the Mahabharata with which we are familiar to elucidate the distinctness of an epic. Simply stated, these two are long poems. The Ramayana has २४ ००० verses and the Mahabharata has verses between 8०००, २४००० and 1००,००० as per the three different versions given by Vyasa, Vaisampayana and Ugraoerava Sauti. They depict the heroic deeds of the protagonists Lord Ram (the Ramayana) and the Pandavas, in particular Arjuna, guided by Lord Krishna (the Mahabharata )

(3) These two Indian epics are known as Ithihasa where the word ithihasa splits as iti-ha-asa and means thus verily happened. Therefore, ithihasa or the epic means history as it truly happened From the above, we conclude that an epic poem is a long poem including history a definition endorsed by the American poet and critic, Ezra Pound that depicts the heroic deeds of the protagonists. Yet another aspect of the epic is, it is a work of art surpassing the dimensions of realism ' (Mary Mccarthy). Thus, we have the dual relation of an epic to history on the one hand and to everyday reality on the other

UNIT 3 WAR IN THE HOMERIC AGE: HONOUR VERSUS HORROR

Structure 3.0 Objectives 3.1 Introduction 3.2 Different Facets of War 3.3 Honour versus Horror the Iliad and the Sequels to the Iliad 3.4 Reconciliation 3.5 Let Us Sum Up 3.6 Hints to Check Your Progress 3.7 Glossary

3.0 OBJECTIVES

In this unit in the context of the ancient Homeric world we will look at war in ancient times and how it differs from war of the present times; we will discuss the different facets of war that bestow honour on the heroes OU and also bring unmitigated suffering and pain to a large number of men and women belonging to the armies both of the victor and the vanquished. finally in the context of the Iliad and its sequels we study the ancient glorification of war and the logical consequences of the sufferings it causes. OPLES 3.1 INTRODUCTION The history of the world is the history of violence and WAT, and the RSITY Iliad remains the original benchmark for our understanding रof war $ human   dimensions. Jose Rosenthal

The Iliad has generated two responses that are antithetical in nature (1 ) an admiration for force that culminates in victory for one of the warring groups. In this context, force is seen as an index of power, strength, courage and merciless brutality (2 an abhorrence for war that devastates both the victor and the vanquished. Simon Weil in her essay on the Greek classic, the Iliad , published in १९४०, holds that the true hero, the true -subject at the centre of The Iliad is Jorce which she defines as "that Xthat turns anybody who is subjected to it into a thing Joel Rosenthal, president of Carnegie Council for Ethics in International affairs comments: The poem invites uS to reflect on the nature of war itself, and the use of force as it shapes our understanding of virtues such AS honor and   responsibility and vices such AS excessive   pride, vengeance and cruelty

From these two comments arise the basic question: who is the hero of the Iliad? (2) Is it Achilles or Hector?
